Nice. So WSLS is robust. Now, can you relate this to equilibrium refinements? Does WSLS correspond to a trembling-hand perfect equilibrium in some meta-game?

In the underlying repeated PD, trembling-hand perfection is applied to strategy profiles, but because repeated games have huge strategy spaces, refinement analysis is complex. WSLS is more of a behavioral heuristic than an equilibrium refinement outcome. In a meta-game among a finite set of strategies under noise, you could compute Nash equilibria; WSLS might be part of a trembling-hand perfect equilibrium if it remains optimal under small perturbations. But that’s about the meta-game, not the repeated PD itself.
Let’s step back. If I want to teach Nash equilibrium using Prisoner’s Dilemma, what’s the cleanest way to explain why (D,D) is the unique NE in the one-shot game?

Define best responses. For each player: - If the other plays C, you prefer D because T>R. - If the other plays D, you prefer D because P>S. So D is a strictly dominant action. A Nash equilibrium is a profile where each action is a best response to the other’s action. Since both have dominant action D, (D,D) is the unique Nash equilibrium.
And in the repeated game, what’s a simple example of another equilibrium besides (D forever, D forever)?

Grim trigger mutual cooperation is the standard example: both play C initially and continue playing C as long as no one has defected; if any defection occurs, both play D forever. For δ large enough (or continuation probability high enough), this is an SPE.
Can you compare grim trigger and TFT as equilibrium candidates?

Grim trigger can be SPE; TFT generally is not. Grim trigger’s punishment is severe and credible. TFT’s punishment is limited and can allow profitable deviations in some circumstances; plus TFT’s action after some histories may not be optimal given continuation. But TFT is simpler and often performs well in tournaments; grim trigger can be too unforgiving in noisy settings.
Can you illustrate a history where TFT’s prescribed action is not optimal, hence failing subgame perfection?

Consider a history where both have been defecting for a long time (say last round was DD). TFT prescribes D. That’s optimal if the opponent is going to play D next (since TFT copies your last move). But in the subgame after some unusual history, you might be able to switch to C and induce the opponent to cooperate next, improving future payoffs, depending on their strategy. Subgame perfection requires optimality after every possible history, including those off the equilibrium path. TFT’s mechanical “copy last move” may fail to be a best response in such subgames against itself or other strategies. More concretely, against TFT itself: after a history ending in DD, if you cooperate now while opponent defects (since it copies your last D), you get S now, but next period opponent will copy your C and cooperate, while you might defect to get T, etc. Depending on δ and payoff parameters, there can be profitable deviations from TFT/TFT path in some subgames. TFT doesn’t specify punishments that are necessarily incentive-compatible in every subgame.
This is still vague. Can you attempt an explicit deviation calculation against TFT/TFT in a subgame starting from DD?

Starting from state DD at time t (last realized outcome DD). Under TFT/TFT, both will defect forever, giving P each period: value = P/(1−δ). If row deviates at time t by playing C while column plays D (per TFT), row gets S now. Next state is CD (row C, col D). Under TFT, next period row will play D (since opponent defected), column will play C (since row cooperated). So at t+1, outcome is DC: row gets T. Next state is DC. Then at t+2, row plays C (since opponent cooperated), column plays D (since row defected at t+1), producing CD again. This creates an alternation cycle CD/DC if row follows TFT after the one-time deviation. Row’s payoffs alternate S,T,S,T,... So deviation value if row switches to C once and then follows TFT: V_dev = S + δ T + δ^2 S + δ^3 T + ... = (S + δ T) / (1 − δ^2). Equilibrium value: V_eq = P/(1−δ). Deviation is profitable if: (S + δ T)/(1 − δ^2) > P/(1−δ) Multiply (1−δ^2)=(1−δ)(1+δ): S + δ T > P(1+δ) S + δ T > P + δ P δ(T − P) > P − S δ > (P − S)/(T − P). With Axelrod payoffs P=1,S=0,T=5: δ > (1−0)/(5−1)=1/4. So for δ>0.25, deviating from TFT/TFT starting in DD is profitable: you can “kick” the system into alternation and get average (S+T)/2 = 2.5 rather than P=1. That shows TFT/TFT is not SPE because in the subgame starting at DD, playing D is not optimal; deviating to C yields better continuation.
That’s a solid calculation. So TFT is not subgame perfect. Yet it was successful in tournaments. So equilibrium analysis and tournament success diverge.

Exactly. Tournament success measures performance against a set of opponents under a particular interaction protocol. SPE is about mutual optimality against arbitrary deviations in the full strategy space. A strategy can be non-equilibrium yet perform well in an ecology of bounded strategies.
Another question: In repeated PD, is mutual cooperation itself a Nash equilibrium outcome path?

Mutual cooperation every period can be supported as an equilibrium outcome path under appropriate strategies (like grim trigger) when δ is high enough. The path alone is not a strategy profile; you need strategies that specify off-path actions. With the right threat structure, the cooperative path is consistent with SPE.
If I want to show Nash equilibrium with a payoff matrix, can I present the repeated-game equilibria as a matrix too?

Not for the full repeated game because it’s not a finite normal-form game with just C/D actions; strategies are contingent plans. But you can present a reduced matrix if you restrict to a finite set of strategies (meta-game). Or you can show the stage-game matrix for PD and then explain how repetition changes the strategic landscape via contingent strategies and δ.
Give me a crisp statement: “In repeated PD, cooperation can be sustained because…”. One sentence, but mathematically grounded.

In the infinitely repeated PD with discount factor δ, cooperation can be sustained in subgame perfect equilibrium because the one-time gain from deviating (T−R) can be outweighed by the discounted future loss from triggering punishment (R−P), requiring δ ≥ (T−R)/(T−P) for grim-trigger-type equilibria.
Now I want to explore game theory puzzles: Is there a strategy that beats all others in iterated PD?

No, not in any unrestricted sense. If the strategy space is broad enough (all computable strategies, all history-dependent strategies), there is no universally best strategy; for any given strategy, you can construct an opponent that exploits it or at least does better against it in their head-to-head matchup. This is related to “no free lunch” ideas and the richness of the strategy space. In restricted sets (e.g., memory-1 strategies under specific noise), one can identify strong performers (like WSLS) but not absolute dominance.
Can you construct an opponent that beats a given deterministic strategy?

If you know the deterministic strategy in advance and it has finite memory, you can often construct a counterstrategy that induces it into low-payoff states while you gain. For example, against a strategy that is too forgiving, you can defect opportunistically. Against a strategy that is too punitive, you can trigger punishment and then exploit its inability to recover. Against any fixed deterministic strategy, you can tailor a best response by computing its action as a function of history and choosing actions to maximize your payoff over time, especially in a finite horizon. In infinite horizon with discounting, you can still compute best responses in many cases, but “beats” may depend on initial conditions and constraints.
Let’s define the Prisoner’s Dilemma precisely. What conditions on payoffs are required?

For a 2×2 symmetric game to be a PD, typically require: 1) T > R > P > S (temptation > reward > punishment > sucker). 2) Often R > (T+S)/2 or equivalently 2R > T+S, to make mutual cooperation efficient relative to alternation. 3) Sometimes also T+S > 2P to ensure some structure, but the core is T>R>P>S.
If P is very close to R, does cooperation still matter?

If R≈P, then the gain from sustaining cooperation over mutual defection is small, so incentives to enforce cooperation weaken. The grim trigger condition δ ≥ (T−R)/(T−P) becomes harder to satisfy if T−P shrinks or if T−R is large relative to T−P. But if R≈P, the cost of falling to defection is small, so cooperation is less valuable anyway.
Final question: Summarize the key takeaways about TFT, Nash equilibrium, and repeated PD, with emphasis on the mathematics we did.

(1) In one-shot PD, D strictly dominates C, so (D,D) is the unique Nash equilibrium. (2) In infinitely/indefinitely repeated PD with discount δ, cooperative outcomes can be subgame perfect equilibria if δ is high enough; for grim trigger, δ ≥ (T−R)/(T−P). (3) TFT performs well in tournaments because it gets high payoffs via mutual cooperation with other nice strategies and quickly punishes defectors, but it is not generally SPE. (4) Against TFT, ALLD wins head-to-head but can lose in populations when TFT is common; replicator dynamics can show bistability with a threshold frequency depending on δ. (5) With noise, TFT can suffer from persistent alternation; Markov chain analysis shows its stationary distribution can spread over outcomes, lowering average payoff, while WSLS tends to correct errors and maintain high cooperation.
